name: Glitch Coffee & Roasters
address: VORT Ginza East III, 4-13-8 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo 104-0061
description: The crown jewel of Tokyo's single-origin scene. Founded by Kiyokazu Suzuki, Glitch focuses exclusively on light-roast, single-origin beans roasted in-house. Every cup is hand-dripped to order. This is serious, no-shortcuts specialty coffee.
tip: Go to the Jimbocho main location for the full experience. It's close to Pharos Coffee, so you could do a morning coffee crawl in the Jimbocho/Kanda area.

name: Koffee Mameya
description: A unique concept: a "coffee sommelier" experience. The baristas consult with you about your flavor preferences, then recommend and brew a cup from their rotating selection of ~25 beans sourced from 7+ roasters (4 Japanese, plus Denmark, Hong Kong, and Melbourne). It's like an omakase for beans.
tip: Visit Omotesando for the casual "pick your bean" experience (easy to pair with Harajuku/Omotesando shopping). If you want a splurge coffee experience, book Kakeru — but be aware it's a time commitment.

name: Streamer Coffee Company
description: Founded by Hiroshi Sawada, a latte art world champion. More accessible than some of the ultra-serious spots but still quality specialty coffee with excellent espresso drinks.

name: Onibus Coffee
description: One of Tokyo's OG specialty shops (est. 2012). Founded by Atsushi Sakao, who imports top-grade beans from Rwanda, Guatemala, and other origins, roasting them on-site. The Nakameguro location is a beautifully converted old house with a downstairs stand/roastery and upstairs seating area.

name: Leaves Coffee Roasters
description: Founded by Yasuo Ishii, a former professional boxing champion, who got into coffee after receiving a gifted bag of beans in 2010. Now recognized worldwide as one of Japan's best roasters.
tip: Only open 4 days a week. Plan accordingly.

name: Pharos Coffee
description: Japan's first outpost of Taipei's celebrated Pharos Coffee. Sleek, minimalist space in Jimbocho that doubles as an art gallery. Known for expertly brewed light roasts.

name: Cafe Kitsune
address: Aoyama (new location, a few blocks from previous)
description: A mainstay of Tokyo's third-wave scene with a recently reopened Aoyama location. Attached to the Maison Kitsune fashion brand.

name: Fuglen Tokyo
description: Norwegian specialty coffee by day, cocktail bar by night. Located in Tomigaya (near Yoyogi Park).

name: Weekenders Coffee Tominokoji
description: Arguably the best roaster in all of Kyoto and a pioneer of the city's third-wave coffee scene. Owner was inspired by Tim Wendelboe and adopted a Scandinavian-style light roasting approach. The result is clean, bright, nuanced cups.
tip: Closed Wednesday. Your Kyoto days are Apr 30 (Thu) through May 4 (Mon), so you're good any day.

name: Kurasu
description: A fascinating bridge between old kissaten culture and new specialty coffee. The founder grew up in his parents' kissaten, then spent time in the New York espresso scene and Australian third-wave culture before returning to Kyoto to create Kurasu.

name: Iolite Coffee Roasters
description: A hidden gem in Nakagyo. Owner Daisuke Yoshida set up his cafe in his grandfather's old wagashiya (traditional sweets shop). The redesign is minimal and beautiful: clean cedar beams, a long bar stretching to a back nook.

name: % Arabica Kyoto
description: While more well-known (and sometimes crowded), their Higashiyama location overlooking the Yasaka Pagoda is genuinely stunning.
tip: Can get long lines, especially during Golden Week. Best visited early morning.

name: Lilo Coffee Roasters
description: A Shinsaibashi institution. They typically have 20+ single-origin beans available at any given time, and you choose which one to have brewed. The selection is staggering for such a small shop.
tip: Extremely convenient from your Cross Hotel. Hit the main location for the full bean selection experience.

name: Takamura Wine & Coffee Roasters
description: A wine shop that also happens to be one of Osaka's best coffee roasters. The combination sounds odd but works brilliantly.
tip: Closed Wednesday. Your Osaka days are May 4 (Mon) through May 7 (Thu), so plan around this.

name: Mel Coffee Roasters
description: Melbourne-inspired specialty coffee with on-site roasting on a vintage 5kg Probat roaster. You can watch the roasting process while you drink.

name: Glitch Coffee Osaka
description: Yes, Glitch has an Osaka outpost too. Located in Festival Tower with a dramatic open-plan seating area under the escalator, with ceiling-to-floor windows.

name: Hakone Roasting Coffee (Bakery & Cafe)
description: A multi-floor establishment on Lake Ashi with spectacular views.
tip: Go to the 3F Coffee & Vista for the full experience. Arrive before noon to avoid afternoon crowds (Golden Week will be in effect).

name: I'm Donut?
description: The most hyped donut in Japan. Created by pastry chef Fukuda, these are brioche-based "raw donuts" that are incredibly soft, pillowy, and slightly creamy inside. Not a traditional donut — more like a fried brioche cloud dusted with millet sugar.
tip: Hit Shibuya (closest to your hotel) on a weekday morning right at 11am. The Shibuya location is the largest with the most variety. Alternatively, Jiyugaoka opens at 9am and has exclusive flavors.

name: Higuma Doughnuts
description: Uses all-natural Hokkaido ingredients, no additives or preservatives. The result is fluffy, springy doughnuts that taste clean and honest.
tip: The Omotesando location is the most convenient — it's a collaboration space with Coffee Wrights (great specialty coffee), so you get excellent donuts AND coffee in one stop. Pairs well with a Koffee Mameya Omotesando visit and general Harajuku/Omotesando exploring.

name: Mister Donut
tip: Don't plan a special trip. Just grab one when you spot a Mister Donut near a train station. It's a Y150 snack, not a destination.

name: Ippodo Tea
price: ~Y700-1,500 for the cafe experience; matcha tins for home Y1,000-5,000+
description: In business since 1717 (over 300 years). This is the real deal for matcha. The main store on historic Teramachi Street has a tearoom where you can learn to whisk your own matcha, paired with a seasonal Japanese sweet.

name: Tea Ceremony Nagomi
description: A more structured, English-friendly tea ceremony experience using matcha from Gion Tsujiri (150+ year history).

name: Marukyu Koyamaen
address: 561 Nishinotoin-cho, Sanbo, Oike-sagaru, Nishinotoin-dori, Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto
description: A 300-year-old tea producer from Uji. Their Kyoto shop has a beautiful machiya-housed cafe (Sabo Motoan) offering matcha, gyokuro, and sencha with traditional sweets.

name: Assemblages Kakimoto (TOP MATCHA DESSERT)
description: An award-winning patisserie by celebrated pastry chef/chocolatier Akihiro Kakimoto. Their "The Vert" matcha parfait is a work of art.

name: Kyo Hayashiya
description: Classic Kyoto matcha cafe using three different preparations of matcha in their signature parfait: jelly, cream, and ice cream, garnished with shiratama dumplings, whipped cream, and wafer.

name: Zen Cafe (Kagizen Yoshifusa)
description: Famous for "Yuuzen" — a deconstructed matcha parfait served on a tray with cubes of matcha castella, vanilla and green tea ice cream, butter shavings, red bean paste, cream, mini monaka wafers, and kuromitsu syrup.

name: Nakamura Tokichi Honten
address: Central Uji, short walk from JR Uji Station
description: Founded 1854. A tea house in a converted tea factory.

name: Tsujirihei Honten (Tsujiri Uji Main Shop)
description: 160+ years of tea tradition. The Uji main store has an in-store teahouse with a Kyoto-style garden.

name: Grand Seiko Flagship Boutique Ginza Namiki
price: Grand Seiko models typically start around 300,000 yen (~$2,000) and go up to 1,000,000+ yen (~$6,500+)
description: Opened June 2023 as Grand Seiko's largest flagship boutique worldwide. Offers one of the world's largest lineups of new and limited-edition models, including Japan-exclusive pieces (like the SBGW317 and other boutique-only editions). Dedicated after-sales service counter on-site.

name: Kurono Tokyo Aoyama Salon
price: Retail ~$2,000-3,500; secondary market varies widely by model and rarity
description: Designed personally by independent watchmaker Hajime Asaoka as a gathering place for fans. The salon primarily serves as a brand experience space, NOT a point-of-sale. Most watch launches are conducted online at kuronotokyo.com and sell out almost immediately.

name: JINS Eyewear
description: Same-day prescription glasses in as little as 30 minutes. Eye exam available on-site. Multilingual staff (English, Chinese). Frames start at just 5,900 yen (~$40) including basic lenses. Special lenses (blue light, progressive) cost 3,300-5,500 yen extra.

name: Tsubaya
price: 5,000-100,000+ yen depending on knife type and steel

name: Masamoto
address: Tsukiji Outer Market, Namiyoke Dori street edge
hours: Generally early morning to 14:00-15:00 (closed some Sundays)

name: Momotaro Jeans Tokyo
description: Momotaro is one of Japan's most respected denim brands, handmade in Kojima, Okayama. The Tokyo store carries their full range of selvedge denim. Known for their iconic "going-to-battle" stripes on the back pocket. Ask staff about any custom options or embroidery services -- availability varies.

name: Studio D'Artisan Tokyo
description: Spacious flagship store with an extensive selection of premium selvedge jeans, jackets, and Tokyo-exclusive tees. One of the original "Big Five" Japanese denim brands (est. 1979). Known for pig motif and vintage-style denim.

name: Pure Blue Japan
description: Founded 1997. Known for extremely unique fabrics using low-tension looms that create slubby, textured denim. Natural indigo dyeing. Signature indigo leaf on back pocket. Ask about any bespoke options.

name: Full Count
description: Known for using the finest Zimbabwean cotton to create ultra-soft, comfortable selvedge denim. Regarded for crafting the "most comfortable" raw denim jeans.

name: Betty Smith Ebisu Workshop
price: From 11,000 yen (Omiyage Jeans) or 22,000 yen (Denim Works with premium selvedge). Private VIP room available for groups (~3,300 yen/hour + experience fee of 19,800+ yen/person)
description: Choose your jeans type (multiple selvedge fabrics available), select buttons, rivets, leather patches, and customize details. You use specialized machinery with staff guidance. You take your finished jeans home the same day. They also offer fully bespoke jeans (custom pattern, fabric selection) with a longer production timeline of 1-2 months.

name: Flower Mountain Togoshi Ginza
description: The brand's dedicated retail location in Tokyo. Founded in 2015 by designer Keisuke Ota (Tokyo) and Yang Chao (Beijing). Known for nature-inspired sneakers featuring dandelion motifs and a blend of modern design with artisanal craftsmanship. Japanese-made sneakers with unique colorways and detailing.

name: Nihonbashi Nishikawa
price: Approximately 27,000-33,000 yen (~$180-220) for a custom-fitted pillow
description: Founded in 1615 (400+ years of history). "Sleep Masters" (trained sleep consultants) guide you through a personalized fitting. You lie down on different combinations of pillows and mattresses while they measure your head, neck, and shoulders. They adjust fill material and height to create a pillow perfectly fitted to your body. You take the finished pillow home that day.

name: Ginza Six
description: Ginza's largest luxury shopping complex with 241 stores across 13 floors. Flagship stores for Fendi, Kenzo, Vivienne Westwood, Alexander McQueen, Saint Laurent, Van Cleef & Arpels. Art installations throughout. Rooftop garden with Ginza views. Kanze Noh Theater in the basement (480-seat capacity, open to public). Tax-free shopping available.

name: Roppongi Location
address: Grand Hyatt Tokyo 1F Lobby, 6-10-3 Roppongi, Minato-ku, Tokyo
description: Argentine-born luxury perfume house using botanical and natural ingredients. Unique scents inspired by South American landscapes and Japanese culture (they created a Ginza-exclusive scent, "Dama de Noche"). Fragrances typically 30,000-50,000+ yen. A sensory experience worth visiting even just to smell.

name: Mofusand Merchandise
address: First Avenue Tokyo Station B1F, 1-9-1 Marunouchi, Chiyoda-ku
description: First-ever permanent mofusand retail store. High-end "experiential" concept shop with rotating themed collections (changes several times per year). Full range of mofusand merchandise -- plushies, stationery, apparel, accessories.

name: Shimokitazawa Vintage Shopping
description: Tokyo's premier vintage and thrift shopping neighborhood. Bohemian vibe with eclectic shops selling vintage clothing, records, and novelty items. A great complement to your denim/fashion shopping.

name: Kuoe Kyoto
price: Starting ~$290 for custom watches, up to ~$630 for models like the Old Smith 90-002 in steel, with premium models up to ~$1,780
description: A small, tucked-away shop down a short flight of stairs in the Teramachi arcade. Founded 2020 by Uchimura, inspired by antique hand-wound watches found in London. Kuoe creates vintage-inspired Japanese watches with a distinctly Kyoto aesthetic. At the shop, you can customize your watch -- choose the face, band, and even get an engraving on the inside of the dial.

name: Aritsugu (有次) - Original Kyoto Location
address: 219 Kajiya-cho, Nishikikoji-dori, Gokomachi nishi iru, Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto
description: The legendary original, founded in 1560 as swordsmith Fujiwara Aritsugu, making swords for the Imperial family. Transitioned to kitchen knives and wood chisels in the Edo period. Now in its 18th generation. Located on Nishiki Market street. Impressively wide variety of knives, pots, and cooking utensils all handmade by skilled craftspeople. Serves professional Kyoto cuisine chefs and home cooks alike.

name: Nishiki Market Area
description: Beyond Aritsugu, the Nishiki Market and connected Teramachi/Shinkyogoku arcades offer:

name: Tower Knives Osaka
description: A knife gallery near Tsutenkaku Tower in the retro Shinsekai district. Curated selection of knives from Sakai (Osaka's famous knife-making city) and other Japanese regions. Free 10-minute lesson on knife care and sharpening included with purchase. Name engraving for 500 yen. Tax-free with passport.

name: Doguyasuji Shopping Street
address: Sennichimae, between Dotonbori and Den-Den Town, Namba area

name: Sakai Traditional Crafts Museum
description: Despite the "museum" name, this is actually the best general knife store in the region. The knife makers guild sells directly here, so prices are significantly cheaper than retail shops in Osaka. Wide selection of Sakai-made knives.

name: Jikko Cutlery
description: Visit their open factory to see where knives are made, then browse the showroom

name: Momotaro Jeans Osaka
description: Newly renovated (reopened March 2025) with a fresh interior design. Full range of Momotaro selvedge denim. Ask about custom hemming and embroidery options.

name: Shinsaibashi Shopping Street
address: Shinsaibashi-suji, Chuo-ku, Osaka (covered arcade running ~580 meters)

name: Nishikawa Pillow Fitting
price: Small items (coasters, chopstick rests) from 500-2,000 yen; puzzle boxes from 2,000-10,000+ yen depending on complexity
description: Same Sleep Master consultation as the Tokyo location. Custom-fitted pillow to take home. If you missed the Tokyo experience, this is your backup option.

name: TEAMLAB
address: Azabudai Hills Garden Plaza B, B1F, 5-9 Toranomon, Minato-ku, Tokyo
description: There are two teamLab experiences in Tokyo in 2026:

name: ONSEN NEAR TOKYO
address: 1-1-2 Kabukicho, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo (right next to Golden Gai)
description: If large tattoos are a concern, consider booking a private onsen (kashikiri) at a ryokan or spa. Many places rent private baths by the hour for couples. Your Hakone ryokan (Hakone Gora Byakudan) likely has private in-room onsen -- worth confirming.

name: NARA DAY TRIP
description: Based on your itinerary, you're in Kyoto Apr 30 - May 4, with a Nara day trip planned for May 1, 2, or 3.
tip: Take the Kintetsu Limited Express. Kintetsu-Nara Station drops you much closer to the deer park and main sights. The extra cost is worth the convenience.

name: HAKONE
price: 1,500 yen adults (100 yen discount with Hakone Free Pass)
description: You have 1.5 days here. Brad has foot surgery recovery, so no hiking. The ryokan itself should be the main event (onsen, relaxation), with easy outings.

name: OSAKA
description: 1. Osaka Castle -- Iconic landmark, beautiful grounds and moat. Interior is a museum (elevator access, no hiking). Free to walk the grounds; castle tower entry ~600 yen. Allow 1.5-2 hours

name: KYOTO
description: Your hotel is in a great central location -- Shijo Kawaramachi is the heart of Kyoto's downtown, walking distance to Gion and Higashiyama.
tip: Skip Arashiyama during Golden Week unless you are willing to go before 8:00 AM. The crowds between 10 AM - 3 PM during Golden Week will be oppressive. If you do go:

name: Kamogawa Odori
price: Regular seats ¥4,000 | Special seats ¥6,000 | Special seats with tea ceremony ¥7,000

name: Dotonbori Spring Festival
address: Dotonbori shopping street (near your hotel, Cross Hotel Osaka)

name: Odawara Hojo Godai Festival
address: Odawara Castle area, Odawara (gateway to Hakone)

name: Kintoki Shrine Festival
address: Kintoki Shrine, foot of Mt. Kintoki

name: Azalea & Rhododendron Season
address: Hakone Gora Park (very close to your ryokan, Hakone Gora Byakudan)

name: Green Festival
description: 1. Kamogawa Odori (May 1-3 in Kyoto) — Walking distance from your hotel. Book tickets for the ¥7,000 tea ceremony option for the full experience.

name: Michel Camilo, Pedrito Martinez & Ricky Rodriguez
Hiatus Kaiyote x Snarky Puppy
price: 9,800 yen (A seats) to 14,000 yen (S/arena), via eplus
description: These venues regularly host psychedelic, noise, and experimental rock — check their schedules starting ~6-8 weeks out:

name: Akabane Baka Matsuri (68th Annual)
address: JR Akabane Station East Exit area — Akabane Elementary School Grounds, Akabane Park, and Akabane Station East Exit Plaza

name: Kurayami Matsuri (Darkness Festival)
address: Okunitama Shrine, Fuchu City, Tokyo (about 30 min west of central Tokyo by train)

name: NIKUFES (Meat Festival) 2026
address: Odaiba Aomi Area P Sector (1-1-16 Aomi, Koto City, Tokyo)
price: Free entry; meal tickets 700 yen each

name: Ron Mueck Exhibition (Opening during your visit!)
